explanation: The first scatter plot shows a negative relationship between late night screen time and sleep duration. As screen time increases, sleep duration tends to decrease. The second scatter plot shows a positive relationship between sleep duration and mood rating. Nights with more sleep were associated with higher mood scores. Although the sample size is small, the overall pattern supports my hypothesis that reducing screen time may improve mood indirectly by improving sleep.

This scatter plot shows a negative relationship between screen time after 9:30 p.m. and sleep duration. As my screen time increased, my sleep duration generally decreased. Especially on higher screen time nights where sleep dropped closer to the low 6 hour range. Compared to Week 1, I had a few more nights with lower screen time, which helped keep my sleep closer to 7 to 8 hours on those days.

This scatter plot shows a positive relationship between sleep duration and mood rating. Nights with longer sleep were associated with higher mood scores, while shorter sleep nights were linked to lower mood. The overall direction supports my hypothesis that improving sleep may improve mood, and that reducing late night screen time is one possible way to support better sleep.

This scatter plot shows a negative relationship between screen time after 9:30 p.m. and sleep duration. As my screen time increased, my sleep duration generally decreased. Nights with lower screen time were associated with longer sleep, while higher screen time nights resulted in shorter sleep. This pattern is similar to what I observed in the previous weeks, suggesting that reducing late night screen may help improve sleep duration.

This scatter plot shows a positive relationship between sleep duration and mood rating. Nights with longer sleep were generally associated with higher mood scores, while shorter sleep corresponded with lower mood ratings. The pattern remains consistent with earlier weeks, supporting the idea that better sleep may contribute to improved mood. | Across all three weeks, the same pattern appears: higher screen time is associated with shorter sleep, and shorter sleep tends to correspond with lower mood.
